source_paper_title: Convergence of Augmented Lagrangian Methods for Composite Optimization Problems
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Van_Hang_2026, title={Convergence of Augmented Lagrangian Methods for Composite Optimization Problems}, volume={51}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0324}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0324}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Van Hang, Nguyen Thi and Sarabi, Ebrahim}, year={2026}, month=jan, pages={591–620} }
context:
Page 18, end of Section 3 (immediately after Theorem 3.9).

The main question that remains open here is whether \(C^2\)-decomposable functions satisfy in (3.14).
title: Equality between semi-strict and strict subgradient derivatives for C2-decomposable functions
background_latex:
Let \(Y\) be a finite-dimensional Hilbert space, and let \(g:Y\to\overline{\mathbb R}:=[-\infty,\infty]\) be a proper lower-semicontinuous convex function.

For \(\bar u\in Y\) with \(g(\bar u)<\infty\) and \(\bar y\in \partial g(\bar u)\), define:

1) The (limiting/convex) subdifferential \(\partial g(\bar u)\).

2) The subderivative (first-order epi-derivative)
\[
 dg(\bar u)(w):=\liminf_{t\downarrow 0,\,w'\to w}\frac{g(\bar u+t w')-g(\bar u)}{t}.
\]

3) The critical cone of \(g\) at \(\bar u\) for \(\bar y\):
\[
 K_g(\bar u,\bar y):=\{w\in Y\mid \langle \bar y,w\rangle = dg(\bar u)(w)\}.
\]

4) The second subderivative of \(g\) at \(\bar u\) for \(\bar y\), defined via second-order difference quotients
\[
\Delta_t^2 g(\bar u,\bar y)(w):=\frac{g(\bar u+t w)-g(\bar u)-t\langle \bar y,w\rangle}{\tfrac12 t^2},\qquad t>0,
\]
\[
 d^2 g(\bar u,\bar y)(w):=\liminf_{t\downarrow 0,\,w'\to w}\Delta_t^2 g(\bar u,\bar y)(w').
\]

5) The graphical derivative \(D(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)\) of the set-valued mapping \(\partial g\) at \((\bar u,\bar y)\), and the tangent cone \(T_{\partial g(\bar u)}(\bar y)\) to the (convex) set \(\partial g(\bar u)\) at \(\bar y\).

6) The semi-strict graphical derivative \(D^b(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)\) (as defined by the outer limit representation)
\[
 D^b(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)(w):=\limsup_{t\downarrow 0,\,w'\to w\,;\,y\to \bar y,\,y\in \partial g(\bar u)}\frac{\partial g(\bar u+t w')-y}{t}.
\]

The paper studies conditions under which one has the identity
\[
 D^b(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)(w)=\operatorname{cl}\Big(D(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)(w)-K_g(\bar u,\bar y)^*\Big)\quad \text{for all }w\in Y,
\]
where \(K_g(\bar u,\bar y)^*\) denotes the polar cone of \(K_g(\bar u,\bar y)\).

A key function class in the paper is that of \(C^2\)-decomposable convex functions. A convex function \(g\) is \(C^2\)-decomposable at \(\bar u\) if there exist a neighborhood \(O\subset Y\) of \(\bar u\), a finite-dimensional Hilbert space \(Z\), a \(C^2\)-smooth mapping \(\Xi:O\to Z\) with \(\Xi(\bar u)=0\), and a proper lsc sublinear function \(\vartheta:Z\to\overline{\mathbb R}\) such that
\[
 g(u)=g(\bar u)+\vartheta(\Xi(u))\quad \text{for all }u\in O.
\]
(Various additional regularity conditions, such as nondegeneracy and dual conditions, are discussed in the paper, but the open question below is about whether the above identity holds in general for \(C^2\)-decomposable \(g\).)
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether every convex function \(g:Y\to\overline{\mathbb R}\) that is \(C^2\)-decomposable at \(\bar u\in Y\) satisfies, for each \(\bar y\in \partial g(\bar u)\), the identity
\[
 D^b(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)(w)=\operatorname{cl}\Big(D(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)(w)-K_g(\bar u,\bar y)^*\Big)\quad \text{for all }w\in Y.
\]
Equivalently: characterize whether the semi-strict graphical derivative of the subgradient mapping \(\partial g\) can always be expressed as the (closure of the) Minkowski difference between the graphical derivative \(D(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)\) and the polar cone \(K_g(\bar u,\bar y)^*\) when \(g\) is \(C^2\)-decomposable.
latex:
Let \(Y\) be a finite-dimensional Hilbert space, and let \(g:Y\to\overline{\mathbb R}:=[-\infty,\infty]\) be a proper lower-semicontinuous convex function.

For \(\bar u\in Y\) with \(g(\bar u)<\infty\) and \(\bar y\in \partial g(\bar u)\), define:

1) The (limiting/convex) subdifferential \(\partial g(\bar u)\).

2) The subderivative (first-order epi-derivative)
\[
 dg(\bar u)(w):=\liminf_{t\downarrow 0,\,w'\to w}\frac{g(\bar u+t w')-g(\bar u)}{t}.
\]

3) The critical cone of \(g\) at \(\bar u\) for \(\bar y\):
\[
 K_g(\bar u,\bar y):=\{w\in Y\mid \langle \bar y,w\rangle = dg(\bar u)(w)\}.
\]

4) The second subderivative of \(g\) at \(\bar u\) for \(\bar y\), defined via second-order difference quotients
\[
\Delta_t^2 g(\bar u,\bar y)(w):=\frac{g(\bar u+t w)-g(\bar u)-t\langle \bar y,w\rangle}{\tfrac12 t^2},\qquad t>0,
\]
\[
 d^2 g(\bar u,\bar y)(w):=\liminf_{t\downarrow 0,\,w'\to w}\Delta_t^2 g(\bar u,\bar y)(w').
\]

5) The graphical derivative \(D(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)\) of the set-valued mapping \(\partial g\) at \((\bar u,\bar y)\), and the tangent cone \(T_{\partial g(\bar u)}(\bar y)\) to the (convex) set \(\partial g(\bar u)\) at \(\bar y\).

6) The semi-strict graphical derivative \(D^b(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)\) (as defined by the outer limit representation)
\[
 D^b(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)(w):=\limsup_{t\downarrow 0,\,w'\to w\,;\,y\to \bar y,\,y\in \partial g(\bar u)}\frac{\partial g(\bar u+t w')-y}{t}.
\]

The paper studies conditions under which one has the identity
\[
 D^b(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)(w)=\operatorname{cl}\Big(D(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)(w)-K_g(\bar u,\bar y)^*\Big)\quad \text{for all }w\in Y,
\]
where \(K_g(\bar u,\bar y)^*\) denotes the polar cone of \(K_g(\bar u,\bar y)\).

A key function class in the paper is that of \(C^2\)-decomposable convex functions. A convex function \(g\) is \(C^2\)-decomposable at \(\bar u\) if there exist a neighborhood \(O\subset Y\) of \(\bar u\), a finite-dimensional Hilbert space \(Z\), a \(C^2\)-smooth mapping \(\Xi:O\to Z\) with \(\Xi(\bar u)=0\), and a proper lsc sublinear function \(\vartheta:Z\to\overline{\mathbb R}\) such that
\[
 g(u)=g(\bar u)+\vartheta(\Xi(u))\quad \text{for all }u\in O.
\]
(Various additional regularity conditions, such as nondegeneracy and dual conditions, are discussed in the paper, but the open question below is about whether the above identity holds in general for \(C^2\)-decomposable \(g\).)

Determine whether every convex function \(g:Y\to\overline{\mathbb R}\) that is \(C^2\)-decomposable at \(\bar u\in Y\) satisfies, for each \(\bar y\in \partial g(\bar u)\), the identity
\[
 D^b(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)(w)=\operatorname{cl}\Big(D(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)(w)-K_g(\bar u,\bar y)^*\Big)\quad \text{for all }w\in Y.
\]
Equivalently: characterize whether the semi-strict graphical derivative of the subgradient mapping \(\partial g\) can always be expressed as the (closure of the) Minkowski difference between the graphical derivative \(D(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)\) and the polar cone \(K_g(\bar u,\bar y)^*\) when \(g\) is \(C^2\)-decomposable.
location_in_paper: Page 18, end of Section 3 (immediately after Theorem 3.9).
open_problem_quote: The main question that remains open here is whether \(C^2\)-decomposable functions satisfy in (3.14).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: A counterexample would be a specific convex C2-decomposable function together with a point \((\bar u,\bar y)\) where the derivative identity fails. While the search space is not finite, one can parameterize candidate C2-decompositions \(\vartheta\circ\Xi\) (e.g., cones, spectral functions, gauge/indicator sublinear \(\vartheta\)) and numerically approximate tangent/graphical derivatives to test the identity on sampled directions.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can represent candidates by code generating \(\Xi\) and sublinear \(\vartheta\), then approximate \(D(\partial g)\), \(D^b(\partial g)\), and \(K_g^*\) using finite-difference/outer-limit sampling to build a computable fitness signal measuring violation of the identity. However, accurately computing these set-valued derivatives is delicate and may require sophisticated numerical proxies, limiting automation reliability.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Hang–Sarabi, 2023) introduces the semi-strict graphical derivative \(D^b(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)\) and shows that for convex \(g\) one always has an inclusion of the form \(\operatorname{cl}(D(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)(w)-T_{\partial g(\bar u)}(\bar y))\subset D^b(\partial g)(\bar u,\bar y)(w)\), and under subdifferential regularity the tangent cone term can be replaced by \(K_g(\bar u,\bar y)^*\). For important subclasses—most notably convex piecewise linear-quadratic (CPLQ) functions and several cone-indicator examples such as the second-order cone—this inclusion becomes an equality, essentially because the geometry is polyhedral after reduction or because explicit proto-derivative formulas are available.

For general \(C^2\)-decomposable convex functions (in the sense of Shapiro’s composite representation \(g(u)=g(\bar u)+\vartheta(\Xi(u))\) with \(\vartheta\) sublinear), the source paper proves a chain rule (their Proposition 3.5) showing that the desired equality for \(g\) follows if it holds for the outer sublinear function \(\vartheta\) and if a nondegeneracy condition holds. However, the paper explicitly leaves open whether \(C^2\)-decomposability alone (without additional nondegeneracy/dual conditions) guarantees the identity \(D^b(\partial g)=\operatorname{cl}(D(\partial g)-K_g^*)\) for all directions.

Related second-order variational analysis works (e.g., Mohammadi–Sarabi 2020; Mohammadi–Mordukhovich–Sarabi 2021; Mordukhovich–Sarabi 2019) provide tools such as twice epi-differentiability, parabolic regularity, and multiplier stability that often yield explicit formulas for \(D(\partial g)\) and thus can be leveraged to compare \(D^b\) and \(D\). Existing explicit computations in nonpolyhedral settings (e.g., second-order cone programming) support the plausibility of the identity in structured cases, but no general theorem appears to settle it for all \(C^2\)-decomposable convex functions. Consequently, based on the provided material and lack of forward-citation evidence, the problem remains open.

sources.title: On a class of nonsmooth composite functions
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces and studies the class of convex functions that admit a local representation \(g(u)=g(\bar u)+\vartheta(\Xi(u))\) with \(\Xi\in C^2\) and \(\vartheta\) sublinear, i.e., \(C^2\)-decomposability, and develops basic calculus for subdifferentials and second-order objects in this setting.
sources.connection: Provides the foundational definition and structural properties of \(C^2\)-decomposable functions that underlie the open identity relating \(D^b(\partial g)\) and \(D(\partial g)\) minus \(K_g^*\).

sources.title: Criticality of Lagrange multipliers in variational systems
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops second-order variational analysis for composite/variational systems, including multiplier mappings and conditions (dual/nondegeneracy-type) ensuring stability and uniqueness of multipliers, with applications to second-order conditions and sensitivity.
sources.connection: Supplies multiplier-mapping tools and dual conditions closely related to those used in the source paper to derive chain rules for graphical derivatives; informs when additional regularity beyond \(C^2\)-decomposability is needed.

sources.title: Twice epi-differentiability of extended-real-valued functions with applications in composite optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes broad sufficient conditions and calculus rules for twice epi-differentiability and second subderivatives for composite/extended-real-valued functions, including parabolic regularity and proto-differentiability consequences.
sources.connection: Provides machinery connecting second subderivatives and proto-derivatives of subgradient mappings, which is central to relating \(D(\partial g)\) and semi-strict variants in the \(C^2\)-decomposable setting.

sources.title: Parabolic regularity via geometric variational analysis
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops geometric criteria for parabolic regularity and related second-order properties for sets and functions, enabling refined second-order calculus and stability analysis.
sources.connection: Parabolic regularity and proto-differentiability are closely tied to explicit formulas for \(D(\partial g)\) and may be prerequisites for proving the desired identity for general \(C^2\)-decomposable \(g\).

sources.title: Second-order variational analysis in second-order cone programming
sources.theorem_or_result: Derives explicit second-order variational formulas (including graphical derivatives of normal cone/subgradient mappings) for second-order cone programming, handling boundary and vertex cases.
sources.connection: Serves as a nonpolyhedral testbed where the identity between semi-strict and strict graphical derivatives can be verified (as in the source paper’s Example 3.6 for \(\delta_Q\)), suggesting how to extend to other \(C^2\)-cone reducible structures.

sources.title: Primal superlinear convergence of SQP methods for piecewise linear-quadratic composite optimization problems
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops second-order analysis and convergence results for SQP in CPLQ composite optimization, using reduction lemmas and explicit graphical derivative formulas for \(\partial g\) in the CPLQ setting.
sources.connection: Confirms the desired identity in the CPLQ case (where the right-hand side is polyhedral and closure can be dropped), providing a solved subclass and techniques that might generalize to \(C^2\)-decomposable but non-CPLQ functions.

sources.title: Quadratic growth and strong metric subregularity of the subdifferential via subgradient graphical derivative
sources.theorem_or_result: Relates quadratic growth/strong metric subregularity to properties of the subgradient graphical derivative, providing criteria and estimates in variational analysis.
sources.connection: Offers complementary characterizations linking \(D(\partial g)\) to stability properties; such links may help in proving or disproving the semi-strict vs strict graphical derivative identity under \(C^2\)-decomposability.
